cards.front: What is a project in C++ development?
cards.back: A project is a container that holds all source files, images, data files, and settings needed to produce an executable. It saves IDE settings and remembers your working state.

cards.text: Each project corresponds to {{c1::one program}}. When creating a second program, you should create a {{c2::new project}}.

cards.back: A console project creates programs that run from the command line/terminal. They have no graphical interface and interact via text input/output.

cards.concept: Best practice for organizing C++ projects
cards.explanation:
Create a structured approach to projects:
  • Create one project per program
  • Use descriptive project names
  • Organize projects in a dedicated folder
  • Keep related projects in subfolders
  • Don't reuse projects for different programs
This keeps your work organized and makes projects easier to find and manage.

cards.text: The classic first C++ program prints {{c1::Hello, world!}} using {{c2::std::cout << "Hello, world!"}}.

cards.question: What does this classic first program output?
cards.explanation: This is the traditional first C++ program. It includes the iostream library, defines the main function, and outputs the text "Hello, world!" to the console.

cards.front: What is the difference between building and running a program?
cards.back: Building compiles and links your code into an executable. Running executes that compiled program. Most IDEs have separate buttons/commands for build and run.

cards.text: In Visual Studio, press {{c1::Ctrl+Shift+B}} to build, {{c2::Ctrl+F5}} to run without debugging, or {{c3::F5}} to run with debugging.

cards.front: What should you do if your program compiles but the console window closes immediately?
cards.back: Use the IDE's "run without debugging" option (Ctrl+F5 in Visual Studio) or add code to wait for input before the program ends. The window closes because the program finished.

cards.concept: Why you might not see your program's output
cards.explanation:
Common reasons the console closes too quickly:
  • Program executes and terminates immediately
  • Running with debugging (F5) instead of without (Ctrl+F5)
  • IDE settings configured to close console automatically
Solution: Use "Run without debugging" or configure IDE to keep console open.
examples: Visual Studio: Use Ctrl+F5 instead of F5

cards.front: What file extension do C++ source files typically use?
cards.back: .cpp (most common), though .cc, .cxx are also valid. Header files use .h, .hpp, or .hxx.

cards.front: If you get compiler errors after creating a new project, what should you check first?
cards.back: Check that you selected the correct project type (console application), chose the right language (C++), and that your IDE is properly configured with a C++ compiler.
